coredata.dc:title: Wettability Alteration in Carbonate Reservoirs by Carbon Nanofluids

coredata.dc:description: Wetting phenomena play a significant role in oil recovery from complex carbonate reservoirs under harsh conditions. The potential to being able to alter the rock wettability from oil-wet to water- or neutral-wet is often considered the pre-requisite to a successful Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) process. Hence, there arises the importance of understanding the (static) wetting and (dynamic) wettability alteration of the reservoir rock when the rock comes in contact with injected modified brines, oilfield chemicals, and/or nanofluids. In this study, we investigate the effect of three Carbon nanodots in high salinity brines with and without surfactant on static wetting and dynamic wettability alteration of carbonate reservoirs. Outcrop Indiana limestone and reservoir crude oil samples were used in these tests. The contact angle of the rock-oil-brine system is measured as a function of the nanodot concentration, saturating fluid in the rock matrix and aging, surfactant concentration, temperature, pressure, and brine salinity. The upper limits for the values for temperature, pressure, and salinity are set to be representative of the Saudi Arabian harsh reservoir conditions. The use of Carbon nanofluids demonstrated a clear shift in both static and dynamic measurements towards a more favorable water-wet condition for EOR. Statically, the contact angle of an oil-saturated carbonate rock exhibited a change from a strongly oil-wet condition to slightly water wet (with more than 50 % drop in the contact angle value) in 200 ppm (0.02 wt/v%) solution of carbon nanodot (CND) in seawater (SW) over 24 hours. Dynamically, a smaller but still remarkable change (a shift in the order of 20 %) in the contact angle is noted for a sister rock sample over the same period when a solution at the same concentration of CND in SW is introduced to replace the SW base fluid. Nanofluid spreading experiments on oil-coated glass slides supported our dynamic wettability studies. The spreading efficiency of the Carbon nanofluid was analyzed using optical and confocal microscopy with a clear and remarkable oil dislodging effect. Zeta potential measurements of the different systems were made to aid in the interpretations. The combined mechanistic actions of the various factors, including reservoir environment, disjoining pressure, interfacial tension, capillary pressure alterations and nanodots interactions with the oil/water interface, contributed to this change in wettability. Our study supports a strong and favorable EOR impact of CND as an effective and economically viable additive to waterflood operations in carbonate reservoirs.

Schematic diagram of the experimental setup used for measuring both the static and the dynamic contact angles at high temperature and high pressure (HPHT) conditions.
Effect of aging of an oil-saturated carbonate rock sample for one day, one week, and two weeks on the contact angle of a crude oil-carbonate rock-seawater system at 298.15 K.
Effect of different concentrations of the CND nanodot on the contact angle of the crude oil-rock-seawater system at 298.15 K. Both Seawater-saturated (a) and oil-saturated (b) samples were tested.
Effect of pressure and temperature on the contact angle measurement of a crude oil droplet on SW-saturated carbonate rock samples in SW brine medium with and without 0.02 wt/v% CND in SW.
Effect of pressure and temperature on the contact angle measurement of a crude oil droplet on oil-saturated carbonate rock samples in the SW brine medium with and without 0.02 wt/v% CND in SW.
Effect of different nanodots of CND, NaCND, and NH4CND on the contact angle of crude oil with carbonate rock (brine saturated) sample with and without 0.02 wt/v% nanodots in SW at 298.15 K.

Effect of CND nanodot on the contact angle of crude oil droplet with carbonate rock (brine saturated) sample in three different salinities (SW, LS, and FW) with and without 0.02 wt/v% CND in the brine at 298.15 K.
Effect of different concentrations of the CND nanodot on the dynamic contact angle of the crude oil-carbonate rock (oil-saturated)-seawater system at 298.15 K.

Effect of (a) 0.03 wt% CND, (b) 0.03 wt% CND + 0.02 wt% AOS, and (c) 0.02 wt% AOS in SW on the dynamic contact angle of the oil-rock-SW system at 298.15 K for 24 hrs. The rock sample is oil saturated.

Effect of (a) DI, (b) SW, (c) 0.001 wt% CND in SW, (d) 0.01 wt% CND in SW, (e) 0.02 wt% AOS in SW, and (f) 0.001 wt% CND + 0.02 wt% AOS in SW droplets (3 μL) on the spreadability experiments of the crude-oil-coated glass plates at 298.15 K using optical microscopy. All images are taken at the same resolution. Scale bar =500 μm.

Effect of (a) SW, (b) 0.001 wt% CND in SW, (c) 0.02 wt% AOS in SW, (d) 0.001 wt% CND + 0.02 wt% AOS in SW droplets (3 μL) on the spreadability experiments of the crude-oil-coated glass plates at 298.15 K using confocal microscopy. All images are taken at the same resolution. Scale bar =500 μm.

Physical observation of the effect of a 25 μL droplet of (a) 0.02 wt% AOS, (b) 0.01 wt% CND, and (c) 0.01 wt% CND + 0.02 wt% AOS in SW drops on the oil-coated glass plates at 298.15 K and 14.7 psig for a period of 48 hrs.

Oil displacement response with SW on oil-coated slides, (a) untreated, (b) pre-treated with a droplet of 0.02 wt% AOS in SW, (c) pre-treated with a droplet of 0.01 wt% CND in SW, and (d) pre-treated with a droplet of 0.01 wt% CND and 0.02 wt% AOS in SW. Pre-treatment and testing are done at 298.15 K and 14.7 psig for 48 hrs each.
Comparison of the rock-oil-brine zeta potential with and without CND at atmospheric condition.
SARA analysis of the crude oil.
Composition of the different brines.

head.ce:title: Wettability Alteration in Carbonate Reservoirs by Carbon Nanofluids

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wetting phenomena play a significant role in oil recovery from complex carbonate reservoirs under harsh conditions. The potential to being able to alter the rock wettability from oil-wet to water- or neutral-wet is often considered the pre-requisite to a successful Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) process. Hence, there arises the importance of understanding the (static) wetting and (dynamic) wettability alteration of the reservoir rock when the rock comes in contact with injected modified brines, oilfield chemicals, and/or nanofluids. In this study, we investigate the effect of three Carbon nanodots in high salinity brines with and without surfactant on static wetting and dynamic wettability alteration of carbonate reservoirs. Outcrop Indiana limestone and reservoir crude oil samples were used in these tests. The contact angle of the rock-oil-brine system is measured as a function of the nanodot concentration, saturating fluid in the rock matrix and aging, surfactant concentration, temperature, pressure, and brine salinity. The upper limits for the values for temperature, pressure, and salinity are set to be representative of the Saudi Arabian harsh reservoir conditions. The use of Carbon nanofluids demonstrated a clear shift in both static and dynamic measurements towards a more favorable water-wet condition for EOR. Statically, the contact angle of an oil-saturated carbonate rock exhibited a change from a strongly oil-wet condition to slightly water wet (with more than 50 % drop in the contact angle value) in 200 ppm (0.02 wt/v%) solution of carbon nanodot (CND) in seawater (SW) over 24 hours. Dynamically, a smaller but still remarkable change (a shift in the order of 20 %) in the contact angle is noted for a sister rock sample over the same period when a solution at the same concentration of CND in SW is introduced to replace the SW base fluid. Nanofluid spreading experiments on oil-coated glass slides supported our dynamic wettability studies. The spreading efficiency of the Carbon nanofluid was analyzed using optical and confocal microscopy with a clear and remarkable oil dislodging effect. Zeta potential measurements of the different systems were made to aid in the interpretations. The combined mechanistic actions of the various factors, including reservoir environment, disjoining pressure, interfacial tension, capillary pressure alterations and nanodots interactions with the oil/water interface, contributed to this change in wettability. Our study supports a strong and favorable EOR impact of CND as an effective and economically viable additive to waterflood operations in carbonate reservoirs.

ce:section.ce:para: Interfacial science is highly relevant to the upstream oil and gas industry. The recovery processes of unconventional and residual oil trapped inside the reservoir are influenced by key factors that include capillary and viscous forces, wettability and contact angle, and surface and interfacial tension [1,2]. Wettability is the comparative adhesion force of two immiscible fluids on a solid surface [3,4]. Commonly, this wettability is reported in terms of the macroscopic contact angle measurement. The knowledge of the reservoir’s wettability helps predict oil production efficiency and helps provide a quantitative estimation of fluids in the reservoir [5]. Roughly, half of the world’s reservoirs are fractured carbonates that are neutral- to oil-wet. Driving the wettability in these reservoirs towards the water-wet regime will significantly boost the spontaneous imbibition of the water phase and support the enhanced recovery of the oil [6]. This is a very challenging task in EOR operations, though. It involves injecting smart water, oilfield chemicals (surfactants) and/or nanofluids to help water imbibe into the rock and displace the oil into the connected, mobile, sections of the matrix. EOR agents modify rock-oil-brine interaction as a means to alter wettability [6]. This interaction depends on so many parameters, such as rock type (mineralogy), pH, crude oil composition, brine composition (salinity), exposure time, and reservoir temperature/pressure [7,8]. Recent studies supported the concept of wettability (contact angle) alteration with nanofluid injection in reservoir and outcrop core samples to enhance the oil recovery. To name a few, silicon oxide, titanium oxide, aluminum oxide, and zirconium oxide nanoparticles proved effective in carbonate and/or sandstone rocks in static wettability experiments for oil recovery.

Relatively, carbonate reservoirs are more oil-wet, more chemically reactive, and have more fractures than clastic reservoirs and accordingly tend to have more complex oil productivity. Karimi et al. (2012) studied the effect of 24 nm zirconium oxide nanoparticles in deionized (DI) water on the static contact angle measurements of the carbonate outcrop samples at atmospheric conditions. Adsorption of the nanoparticles on the carbonate surface contributed to a significant and positive change of wettability in this solid-liquid-air system. They conducted the study at low pH conditions [9]. Safari and Jamialahmadi (2013) explored the effect of 0.5 wt% lipophobic-hydrophilic polysilicon nanoparticles (14 nm) in NaCl solution on the contact angle of carbonate outcrop sample (solid-liquid-air system) at atmospheric conditions and perceived a favorable change of wettability on the carbonate sample [10]. Bayat et al. (2014) ran several solid-liquid-liquid static wettability studies on the effect of Al2O3, TiO2, and SiO2 nanoparticles (20-40 nm, 0.005 wt/v%) in DI water on the contact angle of outcrop limestone samples. Tests were run at atmospheric conditions [11]. Mohammadi et al. (2014) used 0.5 wt% Al2O3 nanoparticles (10-20 nm) in DI water to alter the wettability of carbonate rock samples at atmospheric conditions with favorable impact even in coreflood studies [12]. Al-Anssari et al. (2018) used silica particles in NaCl brine to alter the wettability of calcite substrates at atmospheric conditions [13].

Many researchers also studied the wettability alteration in sandstone rocks with nanofluids. Onyekonwu and Ologo (2010) ran oil recovery experiments in outcrop sandstone samples using polycrystalline silicon nanofluid in 30,000 ppm TDS brine [14]. Though they observed a positive impact on wettability, colloidal stability, adsorption, and formation damage proved problematic in these tests. Li et al. (2013) studied inorganic nanoparticles on outcrop sandstone samples with a remarkable impact on wettability [15]. Giraldo et al. (2013) noted a positive shift in the sandstone wettability using Al2O3 nanofluid and anionic surfactant solution [16]. Zhang et al (2014) achieved 55 % recovery of oil in place in imbibition experiments on Berea sandstone samples. They used silica nanoparticles in deionized (DI) water. For comparison, their imbibition tests with DI water achieved only 5 % recovery of the oil in place [17]. They also noted some instability issues of the Silica suspension with a change in the pH of the medium.

In all these studies, the wettability alteration is performed statically, at ambient conditions, commonly in a solid-liquid-air (instead of solid-liquid-liquid such as rock-oil-nanofluid) system, and more importantly, driven by an adsorbed layer of nanoparticles at the rock surface. Allowing nanoparticles to adsorb to the rock face to alter its wettability is not only costly but also leads to formation damage in the near-wellbore region and reduces the effective permeability and reservoir productivity. In the static approach, the oil droplet is released onto the rock surface only after the rock surface comes in contact with the base fluid. Because of its simplicity, the static approach is considered the conventional approach adopted in the literature for characterizing the rock wettability parameter.

While the static technique measures the affinity of the oil to wet the rock surface in a given fluid system, the dynamic approach measures the capability of a newly introduced fluid system to help dislodge or mobilize the oil off the face of the rock (i.e., alter wettability in-situ). Hence, there arises the importance of understanding the dynamic wettability alteration of the reservoir rock when the rock comes in contact with injected modified brines (designer or smart water), oilfield chemicals, and/or nanofluids. In the dynamic approach, one monitors the transient change in the contact angle (with the introduction of a new fluid system) for a drop of oil resting on the face of the rock. As such, the dynamic approach is more representative of the EOR process in the reservoir. It focuses on dislodging the oil instead of modifying the rock interface.

Dynamically, Wasan and Nikolov (2003) studied the wedging effect of Silica nanofluids (19 nm) on the oil-rock or oil-substrate interface [18]. They described a structural arrangement and self-layering of the nanoparticles in the thin liquid film at the three-phase contact line. The layering contributes to increased disjoining pressure in the wedge and drives the displacement of the oil from the rock. McElfresh et al. (2012) and Kondiparty et al. (2012) made similar observations using silica (SiO2) nanoparticles (4-20 nm) in 8 % NaCl and 2 % CaCl2 solutions [19,20]. They claimed that the combination of the disjoining pressure and the nanoparticle adsorption on the rock surface leads to the observed change in reservoir wettability. Though the former claim is justifiable, the latter (as described earlier) is not favorable from the EOR perspective. Also, the microscopic mechanism of wettability alteration with nanofluids for oil-wet rocks has not been studied dynamically at high temperature, pressure, and salinity conditions.

The feasibility of any nanoparticle-based reservoir agent is contingent upon its ability to survive the harsh conditions downhole. Many nanoparticles are stable both chemically and thermally. Yet to date, it is still rare to find nanoparticles with the proper surface functionality to allow it to sustain the high salinity and hardness (150,000-240,000 ppm) of connate water, the high temperature (100-150 °C) environment downhole and the prolonged exposure to a mixed charge, mixed wettability and chemically active rock surface in the reservoir. In the reservoir environment, these nanoparticles often undergo agglomeration, coagulation, flocculation, adsorption, and sedimentation, which leads to plugging and/or permeability damage. Furthermore, most of the potential candidate nanoparticles are functionalized with some organic surface-active moieties that require a complex multi-step synthesis process that is costly, time-consuming, and hard to scale-up. Most of the studies also used and tested nanoparticles for wettability alteration in very high concentrations over 1,000 ppm or 0.1 wt/v% and most commonly in the order of 10,000 ppm or 1 wt/v%. Hence, they are considered not economically viable for field-scale piloting or application.

In contrast, our study focused on inexpensive organic carbon nanodots, CND [21], and targeted using very low to trace amounts in the order of 200-300 ppm. Whereas their surface chemistry is yet to be fully understood, it makes them chemically inert in the Saudi Arabian carbonate reservoir conditions. Recently, our group studied Carbon nanoparticles with remarkable success for tracer applications in the Saudi Arabian oil fields [22,23]. Carbon dots are 3-8 nm in size, typically composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen. Their nanocrystalline structure makes it inherently fluorescent and highly detectable in trace amounts. They are characterized by their high water-solubility, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, high chemical and thermal stability, mobility in the reservoir medium, colloidal stability in harsh reservoir environment, “one-pot” synthesis and scalability to mass production and commercial field application.

The objective of this paper is to analyze the static wetting and the dynamic wettability alteration effects for carbonate (Indiana Limestone) rocks. We also studied the spreading behavior of different fluids and nanofluids on glass slides coated with a thin film of crude oil. Three carbon nanodot templates were synthesized for this study. Only one system proved to be most efficient and was thoroughly analyzed. The rock-oil-brine contact angles are measured as a function of rock saturating fluid type (oil, brine) and aging (1-14 days), nanodot concentration (0-0.05 wt/v% or 0-500 ppm) in the brine, temperature (25-100 °C or 298.15-373.15 K), pressure (14.7-3,000 psig or about 1-200 atmosphere) and brine salinity (67500-241000 ppm of TDS). The upper limits for the values of temperature, pressure, and salinity are set to be representative of the harsh environment in the Saudi Arabian carbonate oil reservoirs. Temperatures are measured or set in °C and converted to K for consistency. The microscopic analysis of the spreading experiments of nanofluid, surfactant, and a combined nanofluid-surfactant system on the glass slides are performed using optical and confocal microscopy. Zeta potential measurements provided insights for the mechanistic analysis. The efficiency of the nanodots in altering the wettability of oil- and water-saturated carbonate rocks is assessed. And the mechanism for its favorable effects is discussed and analyzed with the aid of the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crude oil, nanodots, surfactants, and brine fluids
ce:section.ce:para: Crude oil samples are obtained from a Saudi Arabian oil reservoir. Samples consisted of 36.2 % saturates, 50 % aromatics, 11 % resins and 2.8 % asphaltenes. The complete physicochemical properties of the crude oil are summarized in Table 1 .

Three different nanodots, namely, carbon nanodot (CND), sodium carbon nanodot (NaCND), and ammonium carbon nanodot (NH4CND), are synthesized and purified using dialysis method [24–26]. Fig. 1 shows the chemical structure of the synthesized particles. A TEM study demonstrated that the particles are 3 to 8 nm in size (Fig. S1). FT-IR spectra showed the presence of a carbonyl functional group (Fig. S2). The Carbon nanodots are highly fluorescent and can be detected at concentrations well below the single-digit ppm levels. The fluorescence quantum yield (photon emitted/photon absorbed) of the nanodots is about 10 % and virtually independent of the excitation wavelength in the 400-500 nm range, making them excellent emitters detectable at relatively low concentrations. (The dispersion stability of the nanodot solutions are screened and appended in Table S1) Kanj et al. (2011) conducted accelerated stability testing at much higher than intended application temperatures on the same materials in contact with carbonate rocks and with the same brine salinities [23]. They demonstrated its stability, mobility, and detectability not only in a laboratory setup over the core lengths scale but also in a field application using full-scale injector and producer wells at distances of hundreds of meters in an active carbonate reservoir.

Saudi Arabian reservoirs contain high saline formation brine that varies in salt concentration from about 2.5 molars to nearly five molars. Injected seawater used in waterflood operations is also highly saline (about 1.2 molars). Consequently, we formulated three different saline solutions for use in this study, seawater (SW) with TDS of 67500 ppm, low salinity (LS) connate water with a TDS of 138000 ppm, and high salinity connate water (FW) with a TDS of 241000 ppm. Table 2 shows the brine composition of the three saline solutions.

Alpha olefin sulfonate (AOS) surfactant was obtained from Al-Biariq petrochemical Industries Co. Ltd. All other surfactants were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Surfactants employed in the IFT experiments were selected from the anionic, cationic, nonionic, and zwitterionic categories commonly used in oilfield chemistry. These included alpha olefin sulfonate (AOS), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) from the anionic category, hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) from the cationic category, Triton-X from the nonionic category, and 3-(N,N-dimethylmyristylammonio) propanesulfonate (Betaine) from the zwitterionic category. AOS is abundant and widely used. It has high solubilization parameters and low IFT. Sakthivel et al. 2019 identified AOS as an excellent foaming agent for EOR application with CND. Many studies also suggest that these surfactants are candidates for EOR at intermediate and high salinities in the presence of divalent ions over a wide range of temperatures [27,28]. Hence it is adopted here for use as a reference for our comparative study with the Carbon Nanodots.

Outcrop Indiana limestone samples are used in this study. Both oil and brine saturated cores are prepared and studied for their wettability tendency in the rock-oil-brine (solid-liquid-liquid) system. A total of 35 disc-shaped rock samples were made. Discs are approximately 25.4 mm (1 inch) in diameter and 3.5 mm (9/64 inch) thick. All samples were cleaned using the Soxhlet extraction method and dried at 100 °C for 24 hrs. Half the samples were saturated with seawater and the other half with crude oil under vacuum and aged in the same fluid at 100 °C at atmospheric pressure for 1, 7, and 14 days. (Elemental and mineral analyses and elemental and mineral compositions of representative Indiana Limestone samples are summarized in Figs. S3–S4 and Table S2 respectively.) Calcium carbonate is the most abundant in these samples. The average petrophysical properties of a representative rock disc sample are summarized in Table 3 .
Clean quartz glass slides that are 46 mm long, 27 mm wide, and 1.27 mm thick were used in the spreading experiments and the associated microscopy studies.

An Interfacial Tensiometer (IFT-700) from Vinci Tech is used to measure the contact angle under high-temperature and high-pressure conditions (Fig. 2 ). In this study, all the contact angle measurements of rock-oil-brine systems are made using the invert sessile drop method. The technique consists of a rock sample holder, a high-pressure cell (with a sapphire glass window), a syringe loaded with crude oil with its needle protruding into the cell from the bottom, a thermostat for temperature control, accumulators for fluid storage, a hydraulic pumping system, a high-resolution digital camera for image capture and a computer system with an image analysis software. The needle is aligned with the axis of symmetry of the sample holder. And an oil droplet released from the needle will land at the center of the face of the rock sample.

A saturated rock sample is mounted on the holder on top of the cell. The cell is filled with brine or surfactant and/or Carbon Dots in brine. As such, the sample is immersed in the test fluid. After that, the temperature and pressure of the system are set. And the system allowed to stabilize for a period of no less than 1 hr. At the start, an oil droplet is released and allowed to rest at the base of the sample. The system is allowed to stabilize again for a minimum of 1 hour before any measurement is made. The contact angle is measured through the denser (brine) phase with the help of the image analysis software. Contact angles measuring at less than 75° are commonly associated with water wet surfaces. Angles between 75° and 105° are representative of neutral wet surfaces. Contact angles larger than 105° are indicative of an oil-wet surface. The experimental study involved three brines (SW, LS, and FW), one surfactant (AOS) in seawater, three Carbon nanoparticle (CND, NaCND, and NH4CND) solutions in varying concentrations in seawater, four temperature settings (298.15, 323.15, 352.15 and 373.15 K) and four pressure levels (14.7, 1000, 2000 and 3000 psig). An optimization study identified CND as the most efficient of the three types of carbon nanodots. The study is based on Zeta potential measurements (see Table S1 in the Supplementary material and the section on “Zeta Potential” below). The study also identified 200-300 ppm (0.02-0.03 wt/v%) as a “potent” enough concentration range for these particles in solution. As such, static experiments were run with and without the AOS surfactant (200 ppm) and with and without Carbon nanoparticles (200 ppm) in brine solution at varying temperature and pressure conditions. Each experiment was repeated three times, and the average measurement is reported.

Also, oil/brine interfacial tension (IFT) measurements were made using the pendant drop method with the same instrument. This was done in seawater with and without nanodots and with and without surfactant as a function of temperature and pressure. In the IFT experiment, a drop of oil is suspended into the bulk brine medium, and the interfacial force is calculated. The interfacial tension is determined based on the equilibrium drop shape and density difference between the two fluids.

In the previous setup, the rock face is exposed to the brine, the surfactant solution, and/or the nanofluid in the cell for 1 hour before the oil droplet is released to rest on the face of the rock sample. Static wettability measures the affinity of the oil to adsorb to the face of the rock with the rock face already preconditioned with the base fluid in its environment. In the EOR process, the rock matrix is mainly saturated (i.e., the rock face is mostly covered) with oil. Brine, surfactant solution and/or nanofluid is injected into the reservoir (the rock matrix) to help displace and mobilize this oil and support a dynamic wettability alteration process. To emulate the latter process, we employed certain modifications to the conventional setup. As such, the dynamic wettability experiment starts with a static experiment, then the fluid in the cell is substituted while the contact angle is continuously monitored over time in this system. The injection fluid (nanofluid and/or surfactant at the desired concentration in the brine) is introduced at a rate of 2-5 cc/min at the base of the cell. The excess fluid in the cell is drained at the top of the cell at a point diametrically opposite to the injection point far from the rock sample. This allowed for a dynamic change of the fluid in the cell while the oil droplet remains in contact with the face of the rock sample. It also allowed minimal hydraulic and temperature disturbance to the oil-rock setup. A manual backpressure regulator was used to maintain constant pressure in the cell as well.

Effluent fluid samples were collected and analyzed for Carbon Nanodots concentration using UV-Vis (HDX-XR from Ocean Optics) and fluorescence (Duetta from Horiba) spectrometers. The concentration in these samples is compared to the target concentration in the injected solution. When the target concentration of the nanodots in the cell is reached, all valves in the system are closed, and the contact angle is monitored over 24 hours. The time-dependent response of the contact angle as well as the pre- and post-injection responses are recorded. Dynamic wettability measurements are conducted at four temperature settings (298.15, 323.15, 352.15 and 373.15 K) and four pressure settings (14.7, 1000, 2000, and 3000 psig) with and without nanodots (0, 0.03 wt/v%). We used the higher value of CND concentration (300 ppm) in these experiments to accelerate the dynamic effect. All the experiments are repeated thrice, and their average values are reported here.

Zeta potential is the electrokinetics potential at the shear plane in the electric double layer (EDL) surrounding a charged particle as the particle moves in a viscous electrolyte solution. A Zeta Potential Analyzer (DT 1202 Acoustic and Electroacoustic spectrometer) from Dispersion Tech Inc. is employed to (indirectly) measure the zeta potential of the different components (CND, Indiana Limestone powder, crude oil, and surfactant) separate and in various combinations in DI water and SW. The analyzer is based on the phenomenon of Electrosonic Amplitude and allows measurements to be made in concentrated brines. The analyzer is not suitable for porous bodies. Accordingly, measurements are made with 0.1 g of milled (down to 150 μm or less) rock sample dispersed in 10 mL of DI water, SW, LS, FW, CND in DI water, CND in SW, or CND and AOS in SW with and without crude oil (5 wt/v%). Samples were sonicated for about 1 hour at 40 kHz before taking a measurement. The zeta potential is (indirectly) measured using a supplied sample holder with an inbuilt probe. The frequency of the applied electric field is set automatically based on the auto-measured conductivity of the fluid. All measurements are repeated thrice to ensure reproducibility, and their average is reported.

A Leica optical microscope (DM 2000) and an Olympus confocal microscope (FV3000) are employed in a qualitative study of the spreading phenomena of the Carbon nanodots and AOS surfactant on crude oil coated quartz glass slides. We used glass slides in this study even though quartz is not representative of carbonate rocks. The glass allows direct observation of the different phases (water, oil, and the substrate). Testing fluids included DI water, SW, 0.001 wt/v% CND in SW, 0.01 wt/v% CND in SW, 0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW and a combination of either 0.001 wt/v% CND and 0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW or 0.01 wt/v% CND and 0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW. The larger concentration (0.01 wt/v%) of CND is needed to discern the response macroscopically. Solutions are sonicated (using Branson 3800 sonicator) at 40 kHz for 10 minutes. Clean glass slides (46 mm long, 27 mm wide, and 1.27 mm thick) are evenly coated on one side with a thin film of pre-filtered crude oil. Slides were not functionalized to improve on its oil wetness. Instead, slides were only physically coated with a thin film of crude oil and aged at room conditions for one day before tested. It is believed that the aromatics and asphaltenes in the crude oil supported a good bond of the oil with the glass slide.

Slides are weighted before and after applying the oil film to validate equal amounts of oil (0.11-0.12 g) are used to all slides. Each coated slide is put in a clean circular Petri dish (90 mm in diameter and 17 mm in depth) with the coated side facing upward. The Petri dish is set on a flat, solid surface and covered ready to be tested. The glass lid is used to avoid any contamination or evaporation of the testing fluids.

Two types of spreading experiments were conducted with the Petri dish laying flat on a solid surface. Initial experiments involved placing a 3 μL drop of a testing fluid on top of the oil-covered slide and monitor the drop spreading performance with optical and confocal microscopes over 1-5 hours. The second set of experiments involved placing a 25 μL drop of a testing fluid at the center of the oil-covered slide, and the spreading effect is monitored over 48 hours. These latter experiments were followed by gently adding 30 mL of SW inside the Petri dish at a point furthest away from the glass slide to emulate a simplistic “waterflood” and monitor the oil removal process off the face of the slide. The added SW will cover the glass slide and interacts with the oil and the testing fluid on the slide. The setup is monitored for an additional 48 -h period. The slide was gently removed from the Petri dish. It was gently washed with deionized water and dried and weighted to estimate the net amount of oil recovered. The latter set of experiments studied the wedging effect of the Carbon nanodots on oil dislodging and recovery from the slide.

Aging was done for up to 2 weeks (at 373.15 K and atmospheric pressure) for oil-saturated samples. Contact angle measurements were made at 298.15 K and atmospheric pressure. Aging in oil of oil-saturated samples improved oil-wetness. The contact angle increased from 160° to 165° over two weeks (Fig. 3 ). This increase can be attributed to the asphaltene deposition from the oil on the face of the rock. Measurements made on oil-saturated samples at the irreducible water saturation, Swi, had reproducibility issues. Aging and sample position in the cell contributed to as much as 5-10% fluctuation in the measured values. It is believed that the Swi bound water at the interface is the leading cause of these issues. Despite this fluctuation, the mean measured contact angle in these experiments was only 3-5% below that of the oil-saturated samples. Accordingly, measurements on only either fully oil saturated or fully seawater saturated samples are reported in this study.

Aging in SW of SW saturated samples yielded little to no wettability change by comparison to aging in oil of oil-saturated samples over the same period of 2 weeks. All rock samples used in static and dynamic wettability testing were pre-saturated (either with filtered crude oil or synthetic SW) and aged in the same saturating fluid for one day before testing.

Fig. 4 demonstrates the effect of CND concentration (0-0.05 wt/v%) in SW on the contact angle of the rock-oil-seawater system at 298.15 K and 14.7 psig. Both brine-saturated (Fig. 4a) and oil-saturated (Fig. 4b) rock samples were used in these experiments. Samples were exposed to the testing fluid (either SW or CND in SW) in the cell for 1 hour before releasing the oil droplet onto the rock surface. Contact angle measurements were made 1 hour after the oil droplet touches the rock surface to allow for the droplet to stabilize. Seawater saturated core samples exhibited neutral-wet to slightly water-wet properties with a contact angle of 82.5° in pure SW. With increased CND concentration in SW, the water-wetness increased rapidly to reach a value of 36.35° at a CND concentration of only 0.05 wt/v% (500 ppm). A similar trend is observed for the oil-saturated rock samples. The wettability in these samples changed from strongly oil-wet (160°) in pure SW to becoming slightly water-wet or neutral wet (75.95°) in 0.05 wt/v% of CND in SW.

Figs. 5 and 6
present the effects of pressure and temperature on the static measurement of the contact angle (for SW with and without 0.02 wt/v% CND) for brine- and oil-saturated samples, respectively. The common trend from these figures is a contact angle drop (or a water wetness increase) not only with the rise in the concentration of the CND in SW (as noted above) but also with either an increase in temperature or an increase in pressure. These have a positive impact on the wettability for EOR. It is also noted that the temperature effect is more pronounced than that of the pressure in the domain of consideration. The contact angle drops more than 50% in the presence of 0.02 wt/v% of CND in SW. The change transitions the rock surface into becoming slightly (for oil-saturated samples) to strongly (for brine-saturated samples) water-wet. The system exhibits a contact angle of 82.5° under atmospheric conditions (298.15 K and 14.7 psi). When the temperature of this system is raised to 373.15 K under constant pressure, the contact angle drops to 73.6° (Fig. 5). Whereas upon increasing the pressure of the system to 3000 psi at a constant temperature of 298.15 K, the contact angle drops only to 78.5°. As such, the contact angle seems to be lesser sensitive to pressure change than temperature change in the domain of consideration. Similar conclusions can be withdrawn from Fig. 6 for the oil-saturated rock cases.

Temperature dependence of the oil-water relative permeability in the porous media is the most extensively studied phenomenon [29]. Even though there are contradictory reports about the effects of temperature on relative permeability, the common trend is an increase in temperature increases the ability of the oil to flow easier than water can. The change is justified by the increase in water wetness, decrease in oil viscosity, and decrease in the oil-water interfacial tension. It is also revealed that with the increase in temperature, the rock surface in SW turns more negative (or less positive), thus, repulsing (or reducing attraction to) organic components in the oil and favoring water-wet behavior [30].

For the brine-saturated rock (Fig. 5), the water wetness effect is smaller with increased temperature in the presence of the CND. The overall drop in the contact angle with increased temperature from 298.15 K to 373.15 K is higher without CND than with CND. This behavior is almost reversed for the oil-saturated rock (Fig. 6). The Carbon nanodots seem to be more proficient in causing a larger improvement in water wetness (drop in the contact angle) with an increase in temperature for the oil-saturated rock than for the seawater saturated one, especially at lower pressures. This is because the brine saturated rock is already slightly water wet or neutral wet, whereas the oil-saturated rock is strongly oil-wet at the temperatures in consideration.

Fig. 7 shows the effect of using different Carbon nanodots, namely, CND, NaCND, and NH4CND, on the contact angle of brine-saturated samples. The analysis is done at 298.15 K as a function of pressure with and without 0.02 wt/v% for each of the nanodot types in seawater. CND is the most efficient of the three types in reducing the contact angle and causing the strongest wettability shift. It improved the water wetness (contact angle) by more than 50% over plain seawater and by up to 30 % over the two other nanodots at the same concentration in seawater. This can be attributed to the unique surface chemistry and functionality of CND and the specific ion effect that monovalent and divalent ions in the seawater play in screening and altering the charges on the surface of the rock, the nanodots, and the oil droplet to favor this wettability change. A complete zeta potential study is provided later in the text to shed some light on this behavior. Also, a similar response is noted with analyses done as a function of temperature and for oil-saturated samples favoring the use of CND over the two other types of nanodots in creating the desirable wettability shift.

Fig. 8 shows the effect of the brine salinity and ionic composition on the static wettability response for an oil droplet on a SW-saturated rock sample with and without 0.02 wt/v% CND in the brine. Tests were performed at room temperature (298.15 K). It is noted that water wetness is more favorable in lower salinity brines. Experiments in SW exhibited higher water wetness properties than ones that were run in either LS or FW. Though LS exhibits a lower contact angle, the difference in wetness with FW seems negligible (by comparison to SW). Increasing the fluid salinity seems to enhance the oil wetting ability onto the rock surface. Most oilfield chemicals fail to cause a desirable wettability change in harsh reservoir conditions of high salinity and/or high temperature. The overall drop in contact angle for all salts with the addition of CND exceeded 50 %. CND generated larger drop (more water wetness) for higher salinity brines with final wettability values for all three salts that are relatively comparable (The drop is larger for LS and FW than SW.) This is a strong indication not only of the potency of the CND as a wettability modifying agent but also of its stability in harsh saline reservoirs.

Fig. 9 shows the effect of varying concentrations of the CND on the measured dynamic contact angle of the oil-saturated carbonate sample at 298.15 K and atmospheric pressure. (The effect of temperature and pressure of these measurements were also performed. The data is presented in Table S3–S4.) The measurements are made 24 hours after the base fluid (SW) in the cell is substituted with the target nanofluid. The response was monitored over 24 hours. Longer exposure times generated little to no variation in the response. The surface turns more water wet (or less oil-wet) with an increased concentration of CND. In Fig. 9, the contact angle drops about 14 % with exposure to 100 ppm CND in SW (a change from 160° to 137.5°). The drop goes up to only 21 % when a 300 ppm CND concentration is used (a shift from 160° to 125.4°). These values are much less than the 52 % drop observed in the static measurement. This is justifiable by the fact that in the case of the static measurement, the rock surface was pre-conditioned with the strongly hydrophilic CND solution before the release of the oil droplet. Usually, this is an unrealistic presumption for EOR application in the field. Accordingly, a smaller change is expected in the dynamic response by comparison with the static response. While the static experiment uses a more simplistic presumption for the EOR application, the dynamic setup is expressively a better and more realistic representation of some aspects of the process.

Fig. 10 presents the contact angle response as a function of time for an oil droplet resting on an oil-saturated carbonate sample following substitution of the SW base fluid with a variety of test fluids. Fig. 10a presents the case for a 0.03 wt/v% (300 ppm) CND in SW base fluid. The shift away from the strong oil wetness is evident over time. This is represented by the changing shape of the oil droplet at the surface of the rock sample. The response is characterized by a continuous shrinking of the meniscus (or contact area) and a continuous increase of the height of the vertex (or tip) of the oil droplet over time. A similar response can be noted earlier with increased CND concentration in the newly introduced cell fluid (Fig. 9). Fig. 10b presents the case for a combined inclusion of 0.03 wt/v% CND and 0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW. The response here is similar to the case of Fig. 10a, albeit with a more rapid and stronger shift. This is due to a steeper reduction of the IFT by the AOS surfactant. In the latter case, the bulk of the shape change took place in the first 3 -h period instead of over the 24 hours of the former case. Fig. 10c presents the case of 0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW (no CND). In this case, the impact is almost instantaneous, with a collapse of the oil droplet on the face of the rock. This collapse is a result of the excessive reduction of the oil/water interfacial tension by the AOS. It also resulted in the formation of microemulsion into the base fluid and a breaking of the bulkier (main) oil droplet. One may question the absence of this behavior from the previous case; mainly, since that case used an identical concentration of the same surfactant. Unlike this case, the base fluid in the last case contained both Carbon nanodots and AOS surfactant in SW. And as will be explained later, the IFT reduction capacity of this combined system is smaller than that of the AOS surfactant alone due to a competitive placement at the interface and a potential intermolecular interaction between each of the CND and the AOS systems.

Understanding the spreading mechanism of nanofluids and/or designer oilfield chemicals on a solid surface is important for the broad understanding of the mechanisms that govern the oil recovery operation. The oil recovery from the complex reservoir matrix and environment underground is hugely under-estimated with this setup. The intention of this study is merely to monitor the ability of a drop of test fluid to break the oil layer, spread in between the oil and the solid surface (glass slide), and help dislodge and mobilize the oil.

The effect of Carbon nanodots in different concentrations in SW and, in combination with the AOS surfactant are studied vis-à-vis the integrity of the crude oil layer on the glass plate. Fig. 11 demonstrates the time-dependent response for a 3 μL droplet of a test fluid (DI water, SW, 0.001 wt/v% CND in SW, 0.01 wt/v% CND in SW, 0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW, or a combination of 0.001 wt/v% CND and 0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW) on the oil-coated slide. The experiments were run at atmospheric conditions of 298.15 K and 14.7 psig and monitored using an optical microscope. Fig. 12 demonstrates the same effects on sister experiments using confocal microscopy. In the latter case, the spreading behavior underneath the oil layer can be seen with enhanced contrast.

Fig. 11a and b demonstrate that both DI water and SW exhibit only a marginal spreading response on the oil-coated glass plates at least over the 5 -h monitoring period. On the other hand, the CND nanofluid appears more aggressive in its behavior, particularly in higher concentrations (Fig. 11c and d). Fig. 12b supports this observation even at very low concentrations of CND (0.001 wt/v% or 10 ppm) in SW. In the same figure, the nanofluid droplet creates an immediate wedge that dislodges the oil far beyond the boundaries of the droplet almost instantaneously. CND seems to contribute to breaking the oil beneath and in the vicinity of the droplet. These characteristics do not manifest itself in any of the other test cases that do not involve using CND. In Figs. 11e and 12c, the AOS surfactant solutions spread only marginally, and they were unable to manifest a similar response well into the 5 -h mark. The combined effect of CND and AOS (Figs. 11f and 12d) shows the highest spreading performance and oil dislodging effect.

Fig. 13 presents a series of macroscopic spreading experiments that were executed by placing a 25 μL drop of test fluid (0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW, 0.01 wt/v% CND in SW, or a combination of 0.02 wt/v% AOS and 0.01 wt/v% CND in SW) at the center of an oil-coated glass slide. The slide is placed into a Petri dish. The experiments were run at atmospheric conditions in temperature and pressure, and the response was monitored over two days. Fig. 13b demonstrates a higher efficiency of the nanofluid in breaking and displacing the oil over the surfactant alone system (Fig. 13a). The spread is largest for the case of a combined nanofluid and AOS system (Fig. 13c). The results from both the macroscopic and microscopic investigations are generally in-line with each other with one exception. AOS in SW seems more potent in breaking the oil layer macroscopically than microscopically. This is justified by the fact that in the macroscopic investigation, we applied a droplet that is almost an order of magnitude smaller (3 μL versus 25 μL) for the same thickness of the oil layer on the slide.

Fig. 14 shows the oil-dislodging response following the addition of 30 mL SW to each of the Petri dishes containing the glass slides from the macroscopic experiments of Fig. 13. Fig. 14a is a reference or baseline case that applies the 30 mL SW to an untreated oil-coated slide. Fig. 14b and c demonstrate a clearer advantage in pretreating the slides with a droplet of CND over AOS in SW even though the CND concentration is half that of the AOS in the SW. More than 87 % of the oil was displaced in the case of the slide pretreated with a droplet of the nanofluid (Fig. 14c). The “recovered” oil amounted approximately to 62 %, 71 %, and 88 % for the reference case, the 0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW case, and the combined 0.01 wt/v% CND and 0.02 wt/v% AOS in SW case respectively.

The same study was performed with different surfactants (AOS, SDS, CTAB, Triton-X, and Betaine) at a fixed concentration of 0.02 wt/v% in seawater. Obviously, in all cases, the surfactant contributed to a larger reduction in IFT than the CND. Surprisingly, the combination of both surfactant and CND in equal concentrations (0.02 wt/v%) in SW resulted in higher IFT values than that of the surfactant alone system. Potentially, this can be attributed to a competitive placement between the nanodots and the surfactants at the oil-water interface, reducing the number of free surfactants that were initially available in that region for the surfactant alone system.

Capillary forces (or capillary pressure) play a major role in trapping unrecoverable oil within the pores in the reservoir. The capillary pressure is the difference between the non-wetting and the wetting fluid pressures at the interface. It decreases with the interfacial tension and increases with the oil wetness. It is also inversely proportional to the capillary size according to the well-known Young-Laplace relation. While CND reduces the oil-water interfacial tension only marginally, it reduces the oil wetness noticeably. The particles do not adsorb to the rock surface and accordingly do not influence the capillary size. The most important factor for wettability alteration, in this case, is the capillary forces. Mechanistically, a layered ordering of nanoparticles forms at the three-phase contact line (where rock, oil, and brine meet), creating an osmotic pressure gradient with the bulk fluid (due to particles motion) contributing to a wedging effect that pushes water into the wedge. A disjoining pressure develops in the thin liquid film of the wedge-like region caused by the short-range van der Waals and other electrostatic forces. The interplay between the capillary pressure and disjoining pressure is critical in wetting or de-wetting the surface. And the disjoining pressure exerted in the wedge at the three-phase contact line further facilitates capillary pressure reduction in the non-wetting fluid and contributes to the additional flow of the wetting nanofluid into the wedge. This means that the pressure of the wetting phase (nanofluid) is increased or pressure of the non-wetting phase (oil) is decreased, by which the wetting fluid spreads faster onto the rock surface than the non-wetting fluid. Therefore, it is demonstrated that the nanodots are capable of altering the rock-oil-brine wettability to water-wet through capillary pressure reduction and disjoining pressure enhancement.

Zeta potential measurements of the rock-oil-brine system were made with and without CND and with and without AOS surfactant. Table 5 shows the data for the carbonate rock, DI water, SW, CND, AOS surfactant, and crude oil in different combinations and concentrations.

We will consider the cases without oil first. The clean carbonate rock in DI water exhibited a very small zeta potential value of -0.96 mV. This is due to the low solubility of the CaCO3 and the low production of CO3 2- in water [31]. CND in DI water exhibited a slightly more negative potential of -6.28 mV. This can be attributed to the hydroxyl (OH-) moieties on the nanodots. By mixing the nanodots in DI water (0.02 wt/v%) with the carbonate rock, the potential becomes even more negative with a value of -7.14 mV. This indicates that both the limestone rock and the CND particles contain negative species. The observation is further reinforced at a higher concentration (0.05 wt/v%) of CND in the mixture with a zeta potential of -7.77 mV.

Similar experiments were performed with SW to understand its impact on the zeta potential measurements. The zeta potential is measured at +18.8 mV for the carbonate rock in seawater. This strong shift in the potential from the measurements made in DI water is mainly due to the interaction of the negative rock surface with Na+/Ca2+/Mg2+ ions in the seawater, forming a strong double layer of positive nature [31,32]. The potential becomes +39.15 mV for 0.02 wt/v% CND in SW, obviously, due to a strong interaction also between the negative species on the nanodots and the cations in the SW. It should be noted that Na+/Ca2+/Mg2+ ions exhibit a stronger interaction with the nanodots (ζ = +39.15 mV) than the rock surface (ζ = +18.8 mV). On comparing the SW and DI water effects, the rock-SW-CND system shows excess positive potential (+53.30 mV), unlike the rock-DI-CND system that exhibited only a small negative zeta potential of -7.14 mV. (The excess potential is further enhanced to +56.87 mV with an increase in the concentration of the CND to 500 ppm or 0.05 wt/v% in SW.) The shift is again attributed to a strong interaction of the rock and CND with the cations in the SW. The wettability alteration of the rock-oil-nanofluids system depends mainly on the interplay of nanofluid-rock, oil-rock, and nanofluids-oil interfacial interactions. In comparison with CND, both NaCND and NH4CND have high negative ζ-potentials in DI water that remain negative in SW (Table S1). The negative potentials in SW would contribute to a stronger interaction of these particles with the rock surface and poor performance in static stability and retention tests. The rock-particles interaction would also limit the availability of the particles at the three-phase contact line and prevent forming an effective wedge to alter wettability with either NaCND or NH4CND effectively.

The last two sets of measurements in Table 5 considered the effect of AOS surfactant on the z-potential of the system. In these measurements, the anionic AOS surfactant is taken in equal concentration to the CND in SW at 0.02 wt/v%. The SW-CND-AOS system shows the potential of +51.39 mV. This value drops to +44.2 mV upon adding the carbonate rock to the mix. The response is counter-intuitive upon comparing with the values measured for the AOS-free systems in the same SW. The drop can be attributed to the adsorptive tendency of the AOS on the rock surface in addition to the excessive negative nature of the three species (rock, AOS, and CND) as it interacts with the cations in the SW.

The inclusion of crude oil in the analysis causes a reduction in measured potentials in the majority of cases (One key exception is the case for CND in SW). This could be attributed to the negatively charged resin and asphaltene components in the crude oil. And the number of positive species (Na+/Ca2+/Mg2+) at the rock surface and the CND are reduced by its adsorption to the oil interface [31]. In particular, the reduction in potential exceeds 50 % in each of the last two cases of Table 5 that include the AOS surfactant in addition to the crude oil. Obviously, in these cases, the significant reduction is attributed to the higher consumption of the surfactants on the oil/water interface than its adsorption on the rock surface. The general trend outlined previously about the ζ-potential values for cases that do not include crude oil remains valid with oil’s inclusion in these cases.

Zeta potential measurements help analyze the complex microscopic interactions of the rock-oil-brine/nanofluid systems, which in turn, help predict the wetting behavior and the wettability alteration process. It is necessary to understand the surface forces between rock-liquids (oil and brine) and liquid-liquid, with the addition of CND. Fig. 15 correlates the measured ζ-potentials of the different systems with the rock-oil-brine contact angle. The difference between oil in SW and rock in SW ζ-potentials, |ζo - ζr|, decreases from 14 mV to 6.8 mV with the addition of CND. This is a clear indication of a stronger repulsion between the two systems and the support of the observed improvement of the water wetness property. Molecular dynamics simulation studies are in-planning to clarify the wetting causal-effect mechanisms further as well as identify the special ion contributions in the complex system involving carbonate rock, crude oil, seawater brine, and the Carbon nanodots.

We investigated the impact of one main type of carbon nanodots on the static wetting and dynamic wettability alteration of carbonate rock samples. Our study focused on the inexpensive and most efficient carbon nanodot type, CND, and targeted using low to trace amounts in the order of 200-300 ppm. Contact angles of the rock-oil-brine system were measured as a function of temperature, salinity, and pressure in levels that are synonymous with the harsh environment in the Saudi Arabian carbonate oil reservoirs. The effect of surfactant and nanodot concentrations were also considered in these tests.

Static measurements followed the conventional pendant drop method. The contact angle dropped by more than 50 % when 200 ppm CND in SW was used in the static mode converting the rock to become slightly (for oil-saturated samples) to strongly (for brine-saturated samples) water-wet. Dynamic measurements complemented the static approach with a continuous (but careful) injection of test fluid (CND and/or surfactant in SW) to substitute the brine in the cell and monitor the time-dependent response of the contact angle (dynamic wettability alteration) for a crude-oil droplet resting at the face of the rock. Changes in the order of 20 % were dynamically recorded over 24 hours with 200 ppm CND in SW. Supplementing the CND in SW with a dilute amount of AOS surfactant supported a remarkable accelerated dynamic, positive shift of the wettability.

A comparative and qualitative study of the spreading efficiency of different test fluids on oil-coated glass slides was done using microscopy techniques. Both microscopic and macroscopic observations equally confirmed the ability of CND in SW to outperform other test fluids in breaking the oil layer and creating a wedge between the layer and the glass slide. Post spreading “slide wash” in seawater provided a first-order comparative assessment of the oil displacement (and recovery) using different pretreatment fluids.

CND also contributed to IFT reduction, albeit much lesser than surfactant alone systems. A 200 ppm CND in SW contributed to a mere 12.6 % reduction in the IFT from plain SW. When combined with an equal concentration of surfactant in SW, it resulted in 7-14 % increase in IFT values over surfactant in SW systems. This can be attributed to the competitive placement of the CND and the surfactant at the oil-water interface when a combined system is employed.

The ζ-potential of different components individually and in combination in DI water and SW was (indirectly) measured. ζ-potential measurements provided a means to characterize the liquid-liquid and solid-liquid interactions with the addition of CND and, thus, provided a good insight into the IFT response and the dynamic wettability alteration process. For CND-based EOR, the wettability alteration proved far more influential than IFT reduction, though both mechanisms are at play. Capillary pressure reduction and disjoining pressure enhancement are the key contributors to this positive effect on the wettability with the CND.

A unique set of functionalities that included colloidal stability, mobility, scalability, ease of synthesis, biocompatibility, water solubility, and inherent fluorescence and high detectability rendered CND an attractive choice for deep carbonate EOR application. Overall, CND demonstrated a strong positive impact on wettability and transpired as an effective and viable additive to the waterflood operations for field use and application.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Mazen Kanj: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Sivabalan Sakthivel: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Emmanuel Giannelis: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.
